pi_items.source: Code analysis: setup_task.sh creates users at 5 days (active_borderline <90), 80 days (active_borderline <90), 180 days (dormant_old >90), and NULL (never). Verifier.py confirms >90 days deactivation threshold. Task description confirms 'more than 90 days'.
setup_task.sh line 50: create_user_with_login 'Charlie' 'Dormant' 'dormant.old@helpdesk.local' '180' with comment 'Should DEACTIVATE - >90 days'
setup_task.sh line 53: create_user_with_login 'Dana' 'Ghost' 'dormant.never@helpdesk.local' 'NULL' with comment 'Should DEACTIVATE'
setup_task.sh line 44: create_user_with_login 'Alice' 'Active' 'active.recent@helpdesk.local' '5' with comment 'Should KEEP ACTIVE'
setup_task.sh line 47: create_user_with_login 'Bob' 'Borderline' 'active.borderline@helpdesk.local' '80' with comment 'Should KEEP ACTIVE - <90 days'

privileged_info_summary: The task uses a 90-day dormant threshold for user accounts in FreeScout, a real help desk application. The test environment includes four synthetic user accounts created by setup_task.sh: dormant.old@helpdesk.local (180 days inactive, should be deactivated), dormant.never@helpdesk.local (never logged in, should be deactivated), active.recent@helpdesk.local (5 days inactive, should remain active), and active.borderline@helpdesk.local (80 days inactive, should remain active). All metadata values are accurate and consistently enforced across setup, verification, and task description.
